summary: Virginia is Mrs. Jones's daughter, about twenty-five years old, tall and slender with a muddy complexion. She has had a tragic past, having lost her child in an automobile accident right in front of their house. Virginia sometimes works at barbecue stands, though not often, and she has shown interest in learning crocheting from me. She also helps her mother by explaining her diet and daily routines, and she cares deeply about the family dog, showing a protective and fiery side.

Tampa is the place where Mrs. Jones orders a special medicine that she believes is essential for her health. Although her doctor forbids her from taking this preparation, she insists she couldn't live without it. This detail came up during my visit when Mrs. Jones explained her diet and medical regimen.

John is one of Mrs. Jones's sons, and he was sent by his mother to help a neighbor's dog that had been hit by a car. Mrs. Jones warned him to be careful because the dog was big and might bite. John is one of three boys in the family, all school-aged, though the older two do not attend school.

Columbus is one of the older boys in the Jones family, around fifteen or eighteen years old. He does not attend school and spends much of his time hanging around stores in Princeton. Like his brother John, he currently does not work and is part of the family of six children.

Edmund is the youngest son in the Jones family, about eleven years old and in the fifth grade. Unlike his older brothers, he still attends school. He is part of the family living in a modest house with his mother and siblings.

Miami is the city where one of Mrs. Jones's married daughters takes her child to dancing school. This daughter is married to an illiterate merchant who owns grocery stores and filling stations, and they drive a new Buick automobile. Miami represents a place of some opportunity and activity for the family, contrasting with their home life.
cues: daughter takes child to dancing school

summary: Princeton is the town where the older boys, John and Columbus, spend much of their time hanging around the stores. They do not attend school or work currently, and this place seems to be their usual hangout. It is part of the local environment surrounding the Jones family home.

The father of the Jones family has been in the Dade County Hospital for many months due to an unspecified illness. Mrs. Jones does not expect him to return home, indicating the seriousness of his condition. This hospital stay has left the family without his support at home.

The Buick automobile belongs to one of Mrs. Jones's married daughters and her husband, who owns several grocery stores and filling stations. They drive this new car and use it to take their child to dancing school in Miami. The Buick symbolizes a level of material comfort that contrasts with the rest of the family's modest living conditions.
